title: Demonym

infobox.value: ([/ˈdɛmənɪm/](/wiki/Help:IPA/English "Help:IPA/English"); from [Ancient Greek](/wiki/Ancient_Greek_language "Ancient Greek language") [δῆμος](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/%CE%B4%E1%BF%86%CE%BC%CE%BF%CF%82#Ancient_Greek "wikt:δῆμος") *(*dêmos*)* '[people](/wiki/People "People"), [tribe](/wiki/Tribe "Tribe")' and [ὄνυμα](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/%E1%BD%84%CE%BD%CF%85%CE%BC%CE%B1#Ancient_Greek "wikt:ὄνυμα") *(*ónuma*)* '[name](/wiki/Name "Name")') or '**gentilic**' (from [Latin](/wiki/Latin_language "Latin language")  **[gentilis](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/gentilis#Latin "wikt:gentilis")** 'of a [clan](/wiki/Clan "Clan"), or [gens](/wiki/Gens "Gens")')[[1]](#cite_note-1) is a [word](/wiki/Word "Word") that identifies a [group](/wiki/Cultural_group "Cultural group") of [people](/wiki/People "People") ([inhabitants](/wiki/Inhabitants "Inhabitants"), [residents](/wiki/Residents "Residents"), [natives](/wiki/Natives "Natives")) in relation to a particular place.[[2]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERoberts2017205-2) Demonyms are usually derived from the [name](/wiki/Name "Name") of the [place](/wiki/Place_(United_States_Census_Bureau) "Place (United States Census Bureau)") ([hamlet](/wiki/Hamlet_(place) "Hamlet (place)"), [village](/wiki/Village "Village"), [town](/wiki/Town "Town"), [city](/wiki/City "City"), [region](/wiki/Region "Region"), [province](/wiki/Province "Province"), [state](/wiki/State_(polity) "State (polity)"), [country](/wiki/Country "Country"), and [continent](/wiki/Continent "Continent")).[[3]](#cite_note-Scheetz-3) Demonyms are used to designate all people (the general population) of a particular place, regardless of ethnic, linguistic, religious or other cultural differences that may exist within the population of that place. Examples of demonyms include *Cochabambino*, for someone from the city of [Cochabamba](/wiki/Cochabamba "Cochabamba"); [Indian](/wiki/Indian_people "Indian people") for a person from [India](/wiki/India "India"); and *[Swahili](/wiki/Swahili_people "Swahili people")*, for a person of the [Swahili coast](/wiki/Swahili_coast "Swahili coast").

A **demonym** ([/ˈdɛmənɪm/](/wiki/Help:IPA/English "Help:IPA/English"); from [Ancient Greek](/wiki/Ancient_Greek_language "Ancient Greek language") [δῆμος](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/%CE%B4%E1%BF%86%CE%BC%CE%BF%CF%82#Ancient_Greek "wikt:δῆμος") *(*dêmos*)* '[people](/wiki/People "People"), [tribe](/wiki/Tribe "Tribe")' and [ὄνυμα](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/%E1%BD%84%CE%BD%CF%85%CE%BC%CE%B1#Ancient_Greek "wikt:ὄνυμα") *(*ónuma*)* '[name](/wiki/Name "Name")') or '**gentilic**' (from [Latin](/wiki/Latin_language "Latin language")  **[gentilis](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/gentilis#Latin "wikt:gentilis")** 'of a [clan](/wiki/Clan "Clan"), or [gens](/wiki/Gens "Gens")')[[1]](#cite_note-1) is a [word](/wiki/Word "Word") that identifies a [group](/wiki/Cultural_group "Cultural group") of [people](/wiki/People "People") ([inhabitants](/wiki/Inhabitants "Inhabitants"), [residents](/wiki/Residents "Residents"), [natives](/wiki/Natives "Natives")) in relation to a particular place.[[2]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERoberts2017205-2) Demonyms are usually derived from the [name](/wiki/Name "Name") of the [place](/wiki/Place_(United_States_Census_Bureau) "Place (United States Census Bureau)") ([hamlet](/wiki/Hamlet_(place) "Hamlet (place)"), [village](/wiki/Village "Village"), [town](/wiki/Town "Town"), [city](/wiki/City "City"), [region](/wiki/Region "Region"), [province](/wiki/Province "Province"), [state](/wiki/State_(polity) "State (polity)"), [country](/wiki/Country "Country"), and [continent](/wiki/Continent "Continent")).[[3]](#cite_note-Scheetz-3) Demonyms are used to designate all people (the general population) of a particular place, regardless of ethnic, linguistic, religious or other cultural differences that may exist within the population of that place. Examples of demonyms include *Cochabambino*, for someone from the city of [Cochabamba](/wiki/Cochabamba "Cochabamba"); [Indian](/wiki/Indian_people "Indian people") for a person from [India](/wiki/India "India"); and *[Swahili](/wiki/Swahili_people "Swahili people")*, for a person of the [Swahili coast](/wiki/Swahili_coast "Swahili coast").

Many demonyms function both [endonymically and exonymically](/wiki/Endonym_and_exonym "Endonym and exonym") (used by the referents themselves or by outsiders); others function only in one of those ways.

As a sub-field of [anthroponymy](/wiki/Anthroponymy "Anthroponymy"), the study of demonyms is called *demonymy* or *demonymics*.

Since they are referring to territorially defined groups of people, demonyms are [semantically](/wiki/Semantics "Semantics") different from [ethnonyms](/wiki/Ethnonym "Ethnonym") (names of [ethnic groups](/wiki/Ethnicity "Ethnicity")). In the [English language](/wiki/English_language "English language"), there are many [polysemic](/wiki/Polysemy "Polysemy") words that have several meanings (including demonymic and ethnonymic uses), and therefore a particular use of any such word depends on the context. For example, the word *Thai* may be used as a demonym, designating any inhabitant of [Thailand](/wiki/Thailand "Thailand"), while the same word may also be used as an ethnonym, designating members of the [Thai people](/wiki/Thai_people "Thai people"). Conversely, some groups of people may be associated with multiple demonyms. For example, a native of the [United Kingdom](/wiki/United_Kingdom "United Kingdom") may be called a *[British person](/wiki/British_people "British people")*, a *Briton* or, informally, a *Brit*.

Some demonyms may have several meanings. For example, the demonym *Macedonians* may refer to the population of [North Macedonia](/wiki/North_Macedonia "North Macedonia"), or more generally to the entire population of the [region of Macedonia](/wiki/Macedonia_(region) "Macedonia (region)"), or to the [Greek](/wiki/Macedonians_(Greeks) "Macedonians (Greeks)") people of the [Greek region of Macedonia](/wiki/Macedonia_(Greece) "Macedonia (Greece)"). In some languages, a demonym may be borrowed from another language as a nickname or descriptive adjective for a group of people: for example, *Québécois*, *Québécoise (female)* is commonly used in English for a native of the province or city of [Quebec](/wiki/Quebec "Quebec") (though *Quebecer*, *Quebecker* are also available).

In English, demonyms are always [capitalized](/wiki/Capitalization "Capitalization").[[4]](#cite_note-4)

Often, demonyms are the same as the adjectival form of the place, e.g. *[Egyptian](/wiki/Egyptians "Egyptians")*, *[Japanese](/wiki/Japanese_people "Japanese people")*, or *[Greek](/wiki/Greeks "Greeks")*. However, they are not necessarily the same, as exemplified by Spanish instead of Spaniard or British instead of Briton.[[5]](#cite_note-5)

English commonly uses national demonyms such as *Brazilian* or *Algerian*, while the usage of local demonyms such as *[Chicagoan](/wiki/Chicago "Chicago")*, *[Okie](/wiki/Okie "Okie")* or *[Parisian](/wiki/Paris "Paris")* is less common. Many local demonyms are rarely used and many places, especially smaller towns and cities, lack a commonly used and accepted demonym altogether.[[6]](#cite_note-6)[[7]](#cite_note-7)[[8]](#cite_note-8)

## Etymology

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Demonym&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Etymology")]

*[National Geographic](/wiki/National_Geographic "National Geographic")* attributes the term *demonym* to [Merriam-Webster](/wiki/Merriam-Webster "Merriam-Webster") editor [Paul Dickson](/wiki/Paul_Dickson_(writer) "Paul Dickson (writer)") in a work from 1990.[[9]](#cite_note-9) The word did not appear for nouns, adjectives, and verbs derived from geographical names in the Merriam-Webster Collegiate Dictionary nor in prominent style manuals such as the *[Chicago Manual of Style](/wiki/The_Chicago_Manual_of_Style "The Chicago Manual of Style")*. It was subsequently popularized in this sense in 1997 by Dickson in his book *Labels for Locals*.[[10]](#cite_note-10) However, in *What Do You Call a Person From...? A Dictionary of Resident Names* (the first edition of *Labels for Locals*)[[11]](#cite_note-11) Dickson attributed the term to George H. Scheetz, in his *Names' Names: A Descriptive and Prescriptive Onymicon* (1988),[[3]](#cite_note-Scheetz-3) which is apparently where the term first appears. The term may have been fashioned after *demonymic*, which the *[Oxford English Dictionary](/wiki/Oxford_English_Dictionary "Oxford English Dictionary")* defines, as the name of an [Athenian](/wiki/Athenian "Athenian") [citizen](/wiki/Citizen "Citizen") according to the [deme](/wiki/Deme "Deme") to which the citizen belongs, with its first use traced to 1893.[[12]](#cite_note-OED-12)[[13]](#cite_note-13)

## Suffixation

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Demonym&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Suffixation")]

Several linguistic elements are used to create demonyms in the [English language](/wiki/English_language "English language"). The most common is to add a [suffix](/wiki/Affix "Affix") to the end of the location name, slightly modified in some instances. These may resemble [Late Latin](/wiki/Late_Latin "Late Latin"), [Semitic](/wiki/Semitic_languages "Semitic languages"), [Celtic](/wiki/Celtic_languages "Celtic languages"), or [Germanic](/wiki/Germanic_languages "Germanic languages") suffixes, such as *-(a)n*, *-ian*, *-anian*, *-nian*, *-in(e)*, *-a(ñ/n)o/a*, *-e(ñ/n)o/a*, *-i(ñ/n)o/a*, *-ite*, *-(e)r*, *-(i)sh*, *-ene*, *-ensian*, *-ard*, *-ese*, *-nese*, *-lese*, *-i(e)*, *-i(ya)*, *-iot*, *-iote*, *-k*, *-asque*, *-(we)gian*, *-onian*, *-vian*, *-ois(e)*, or *-ais(e)*.

## Prefixation

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Demonym&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Prefixation")]

It is much rarer to find demonyms created with a prefix. Mostly they are from Africa and the Pacific, and are not generally known or used outside the country concerned. In much of East Africa, a person of a particular ethnic group will be denoted by a prefix. For example, a person of the [Luba people](/wiki/Luba_people "Luba people") would be a Muluba, the plural form Baluba, and the language, [Kiluba](/wiki/Kiluba "Kiluba") or [Tshiluba](/wiki/Tshiluba "Tshiluba"). Similar patterns with minor variations in the prefixes exist throughout on a tribal level. And Fijians who are indigenous Fijians are known as Kaiviti (Viti being the Fijian name for [Fiji](/wiki/Fiji "Fiji")). On a country level:

* [Botswana](/wiki/Botswana "Botswana") → Motswana (singular), Batswana (plural)
* [Burundi](/wiki/Burundi "Burundi") → Umurundi (singular), Abarundi (plural)
* [Eswatini](/wiki/Eswatini "Eswatini") → Liswati (singular), Emaswati (plural)
* [Lesotho](/wiki/Lesotho "Lesotho") → Mosotho (singular), Basotho (plural)

## Non-standard examples

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Demonym&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Non-standard examples")]

Demonyms may also not conform to the underlying naming of a particular place, but instead arise out of historical or cultural particularities that become associated with its denizens. In the United States such demonyms frequently become associated with regional pride such as "*Burqueño*" and the feminine "*Burqueña*" of [Albuquerque](/wiki/Albuquerque "Albuquerque"),[[23]](#cite_note-White_2020-24) or with the mascots of intercollegiate sports teams of the [state university system](/wiki/State_university_system "State university system"), take for example the [sooner](/wiki/Sooners "Sooners") of [Oklahoma](/wiki/Oklahoma "Oklahoma") and the [Oklahoma Sooners](/wiki/Oklahoma_Sooners "Oklahoma Sooners").[[24]](#cite_note-Oklahoma_2013-25)

## Fiction

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Demonym&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Fiction")]

Literature and science fiction have created a wealth of gentilics that are not directly associated with a cultural group. These will typically be formed using the standard models above. Examples include *[Martian](/wiki/Martian "Martian")* for hypothetical people of [Mars](/wiki/Mars "Mars") (credited to scientist [Percival Lowell](/wiki/Percival_Lowell "Percival Lowell")), *Gondorian* for the people of [Tolkien](/wiki/Tolkien "Tolkien")'s fictional land of [Gondor](/wiki/Gondor "Gondor"), and *Atlantean* for [Plato](/wiki/Plato "Plato")'s island [Atlantis](/wiki/Atlantis "Atlantis").

Other science fiction examples include *[Jovian](/wiki/Jovian_(fiction) "Jovian (fiction)")* for those of [Jupiter](/wiki/Jupiter "Jupiter") or its moons and *[Venusian](/wiki/Venusians "Venusians")* for those of [Venus](/wiki/Venus "Venus"). Fictional aliens refer to the inhabitants of Earth as *[Earthling](/wiki/Earthling_(science_fiction) "Earthling (science fiction)")* (from the [diminutive](/wiki/Diminutive "Diminutive") *-ling*, ultimately from [Old English](/wiki/Old_English "Old English") *-ing* meaning "descendant"), as well as *[Terran](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/Terran "wikt:Terran")*, *Terrene*, *Tellurian*, *Earther*, *Earthican*, *Terrestrial*, and *Solarian* (from *Sol*, the sun).

Fantasy literature which involves other worlds or other lands also has a rich supply of gentilics. Examples include *Lilliputians* and *Brobdingnagians*, from the islands of [Lilliput](/wiki/Lilliput_and_Blefuscu "Lilliput and Blefuscu") and [Brobdingnag](/wiki/Brobdingnag "Brobdingnag") in the satire *[Gulliver's Travels](/wiki/Gulliver%27s_Travels "Gulliver's Travels")*.

In a few cases, where a linguistic background has been [constructed](/wiki/Constructed_language "Constructed language"), non-standard gentilics are formed (or the eponyms back-formed). Examples include Tolkien's *[Rohirrim](/wiki/Rohirrim "Rohirrim")* (from [Rohan](/wiki/Rohan_(Middle-earth) "Rohan (Middle-earth)")), the *[Star Trek](/wiki/Star_Trek "Star Trek")* franchise's *[Klingons](/wiki/Klingon "Klingon")* (with various names for their homeworld), and the [Sangheili](/wiki/Sangheili "Sangheili") from the *[Halo](/wiki/Halo_(franchise) "Halo (franchise)")* franchise, (also known as Elites in the game by humans, as well as players) named after their homeworld of Sanghelios. In the books by Frank Herbert, the people from the planet Dune (or Arrakis) are the [Freman](/wiki/Fremen "Fremen"), but they are neither a nation nor a country.
